title: Whether and how to cover a compost heap
subtitle: What uncovered heaps lose and what the best cover materials are
excerpt: An uncovered compost heap in the UK rain loses nitrogen through leaching, gets too wet for good bacterial activity, and breaks down more slowly. A cover retains heat and moisture at the right level. The best materials are old carpet, a fitted plastic lid, or a sheet of cardboard weighted down. The method matters less than whether it happens.
sourceNotes: Garden Organic composting guides. HDRA composting technique notes (archive). RHS guidance on compost heap management. Soil biology and nitrogen cycling research (Brady and Weil, The Nature and Properties of Soils, 2019).

content.text: Rain falling directly onto a compost heap washes nitrogen out through leaching. A wet heap also becomes anaerobic: the bacteria that break down organic matter need oxygen, and a saturated heap compresses and excludes air. In the UK, where it rains throughout the year, an uncovered heap is usually wetter than optimal. Covering does not mean sealing: the heap still needs to breathe.
Nitrogen: rain washes out ammonia and nitrates. A heap left uncovered for one winter loses 20 to 40 percent of its nitrogen by leaching.
Heat: rain cools the top of an active heap, slowing decomposition at the surface.
Weed seeds: without warmth and moisture management, the heap may not reach the temperatures needed to kill weed seeds.

Old carpet (wool or cotton only): heavy enough to stay in place, breathable, easy to lift and replace. Synthetic carpet is a poor substitute: it does not breathe and microplastics shed into the compost.
Fitted plastic lid: most proprietary compost bins have a lid. Use it. Lift the lid occasionally (every week or two) to check moisture level and add material.
Cardboard sheets: free and breathable, weighted down with stones or timber. Breaks down and can be added to the heap when it falls apart. Replace every six to eight weeks.
Polythene sheet: effective at keeping rain off but blocks airflow if sealed tight. Leave the edges loose or add stones to hold the edges while leaving a gap for air.

Squeeze a handful of compost. It should feel like a wrung-out sponge: moist but not dripping. If it drips water, the heap is too wet: add dry carbon material (cardboard, straw, dried leaves) and lift the cover for a day to let moisture out. If it crumbles apart with no moisture, it is too dry: add a watering can of water, mix, and re-cover.

Remove the cover in dry summer weather when the heap needs moisture from dew and light rain to stay active. In hot dry spells, the cover can cause the heap to dry out faster than it would uncovered. Check moisture weekly from June to August and water if needed.
